content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 31,
1994
Michael F.
Kotowski
211 Calle Serrento
Goleta,
CA
93117
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-94-344
Dear Mr.
Kotowski:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ocilesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
You are an employee of the Santa Barbara City Fire Department
and a shareholder and director of HazTech Systems, Inc.,
a private
business entity.
Does the Act prohibit the Santa Barbara City
Fire Department from contracting to obtain the HazCatChemical
Identification System and training from HazTech Systems,
Inc.?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prohibit the Santa Barbara City.Fire
Department from contracting with HazTech Systems,
Inc. to obtain
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-94-344
Page
2
the HazCat Chemical Identification System and connected training.
However, you are prohibited from participating in or influencing
the decision to enter into the contract.
FACTS
You are an employee of the Santa Barbara City Fire Department
(the “department”).
One of your responsibilities as an employee
of the Santa Barbara City Fire Department is that of Hazardous
Materials Response Team Coordinator.
In your capacity as team
coordinator, you direct the operations of the Hazardous Materials
Response Team under the supervision of a superior officer.
You do
not have the authority to make purchases of equipment or services
for the city,
nor do you have the authority to approve vendors of
the city.
In your private capacity,
you are a part—owner and member of
the board of directors for HazTech Systems,
Inc
(HazTech).
HazTech developed and now markets the HazCat Chemical
Identification System,
a hazardous material identifier kit that is
used in the field by fire departments.
You stated that HazCat is
the only commercially available product of its kind and is used by
most major fire departments, the United States Environmental
Protection Agency,
and all five branches of the military.
The Fire Department currently has a contract with HazTech for
a HazCat and for connected training.
The department is currently
considering renewing the contract with HazTech.
ANALYSIS
The Act was adopted by the voters of California in 1974.
The
purpose for the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, would
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from any bias
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
In our telephone discussion of
October 18,
1994,
you stated that you are an employee with the
fire department,
and as such you were designated in the conflict
of interest code of the agency.
As a designated employee of the
agency,
you are a “public official.”
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the


Our File No.
1—94—344
Page
3
official or a member of his or her iiumediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
You stated that you are a shareholder and member of the board
of directors for HazTech.
Thus, according to Section 87103(a)
and
(d), you have an economic interest in HazTech,
and may not
participate in any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on HazTech.
3
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Clearly,
a decision to purchase HazTech products would
foreseeably affect HazTech.
In addition, pursuant to Regulation
18702.1(a) (2), where HazTech is directly involved in a decision
before your agency, the effect is deemed to be material.
4
In cases where you do have a conflict of interest, you are
prohibited from making the decision, participating in the decision
and influencing the decision.
Regulation 18700.1(a) provides that
with regard to a governmental decision which is within or before
Please note,
our analysis is limited to your financial interest
in HazTech.
If you own 10 percent or more of HazTech, you may
also have an economic interest in sources of income to HazTech.
We have enclosed some general materials regarding other potential
conflicts of interest for your information.
Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a person or business entity
is directly involved in a decision before an official’s agency
when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request;
(2) is a
named party in the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency,
or
(3) is the subject of the
proceeding because the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to,
or contract with, the subject person or business
entity.


Our File No.
I—94---344
Page
4
the official’s agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the official’s agency,
“the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if,
for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.”
Once a final decision has been made with respect to the
purchase of HazTech’s product,
your conduct will no longer be
considered “influencing” that decision.
This assumes that you are
not involved in other decisions that will materially affect
HazTech.
In cases where HazTech is not directly involved in a
decision of the fire department,
as set forth above,
HazTech may
be indirectly materially affected by a decision.
You should apply
the applicable provision of Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed)
to
determine if the effect on HazTech will be material.
Please note, while the Act does not prohibit the fire
department from purchasing products from HazTech,
as we discussed
on October 18,
1994, other provisions of the law outside of the
Act may apply,
such as Section 1090.
You should contact the
Attorney General’s Office for information pertaining to Section
1090.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
enera1 Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
ENCLOSURES
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.
